RESTRICTED / FOR TEST PURPOSES ONLY
DEFENCE ELECTRONICS & WILDLIFE TELEMETRY LAB (DEWT)
DEFENCE RESEARCH & DEVELOPMENT ORGANISATION, NEW DELHI
Evaluation of Edge-Compute Language Models for Secure
Autonomous Field Communication Baselines
Dr. A. K. Sundaram , Scientific Officer 'G'
P. Rajivan , Scientist 'E'
Tactical Communication Systems Division (TCSD), DEWT, India
ABSTRACT— This research paper evaluates the performance profiles and inference behavior of
highly compact, sub-2B parameter Large Language Models (LLMs)—specifically examining
architectural layers analogous to TinyLlama-1.1B—for deployment on low-power, edge-compute
architectures in communications-denied environments. Tactical operations necessitate highly

TinyLlama-1.1B—for deployment on low-power, edge-compute
architectures in communications-denied environments. Tactical operations necessitate highly
accurate, zero-latency localized natural language parsing without external network dependency. We
present a standardized benchmark dataset framework measuring token throughput velocity ( ψ),
perplexity variance ( ΔP), and hardware memory footprint preservation across resource-constrained
embedded systems. Preliminary experimental trials demonstrate that optimized quantization models
yield a deterministic command-parsing accuracy framework exceeding 94.2% while retaining a
thermal and power usage profile compliant with standardized tactical battery packs.

and-parsing accuracy framework exceeding 94.2% while retaining a
thermal and power usage profile compliant with standardized tactical battery packs. 
Keywords— Edge AI, TinyLlama, Tactical Communication, Quantization, Low-Power Embedded Systems,
Natural Language Parsing. 
1. INTRODUCTION
Modern tactical battlefield operational matrices demand resilient, decoupled computing frameworks where
semantic processing can occur without relying on active satellite data links. Traditional deep-learning language
architectures requiring server-grade graphical compute nodes fail to fulfill localized weight-distribution parameters.
Consequently, optimizing small language models (SLMs) with parameters ranging from 1.0 billion to 1.5 billion
scales is critical for military applications.

uently, optimizing small language models (SLMs) with parameters ranging from 1.0 billion to 1.5 billion
scales is critical for military applications.
This paper systematically assesses the structural adaptability of TinyLlama-styled layers when operating under
stringent memory caps. By reducing precision through 4-bit and 8-bit quantization protocols, our system processes
natural language mission instructions at the absolute edge, directly inside tactical transceivers or rugged uncrewed
aerial vehicle (UA V) base stations.
2. MATHEMATICAL MODELING AND QUANTIZATION
To reduce systemic memory allocation overhead, we implement a uniform asymmetric quantization matrix that
transforms 32-bit floating-point weights ( Wfp32) down to an integer-based low-bit format ( Wint). The scaling

rm asymmetric quantization matrix that
transforms 32-bit floating-point weights ( Wfp32) down to an integer-based low-bit format ( Wint). The scaling
coefficient adjustment sequence is represented explicitly via the following formulation:
DRDO Technical Report TR-2026-DEWT-089 1

(1)
(2)Wint = clip ⌊ ( Wfp32 × S ) + Z ⌉
Where S denotes the specific real-value quantization scaling multiplier factor, Z represents the integer zero-point
offset mapping baseline value, and the clipping operation bounds the resultant integer stream strictly between the
target integer min/max distribution parameters.
The network loss function divergence is evaluated continuously by monitoring the relative perplexity metric
delta, formulated as follows:
P(X) = exp ( − 1 / N × Σi=1N log P(xi | x) )

nce is evaluated continuously by monitoring the relative perplexity metric
delta, formulated as follows:
P(X) = exp ( − 1 / N × Σi=1N log P(xi | x) )
An increase in P(X) corresponds directly to a degradation of structural text generation coherence, which we seek to
minimize across token processing operations.
3. EXPERIMENTAL ARCHITECTURE AND BENCHMARK METRICS
Hardware testing profiles utilize an ARM Cortex-A72 quad-core processing matrix configured to mimic
tactical field computing terminals. Table 1 outlines the exact performance matrix captured during standardized test
loops using synthetic parsing commands.
TABLE 1: PERFORMANCE MATRIX FOR LOW-PARAMETER EDGE LANGUAGE MODELS
Model Format /
ConfigurationQuantization
BitrateThroughput Token/s
(ψ)Perplexity Core
ScoreRAM Usage
(MB)

TRIX FOR LOW-PARAMETER EDGE LANGUAGE MODELS
Model Format /
ConfigurationQuantization
BitrateThroughput Token/s
(ψ)Perplexity Core
ScoreRAM Usage
(MB)
Baseline Native Format
(FP32)32-bit 4.2 7.34 4400 MB
Optimized Quantized (INT8) 8-bit 18.9 7.51 1150 MB
Tactical Deployable (INT4) 4-bit 31.4 8.12 620 MB
The empirical findings reveal that while the 4-bit quantization layout suffers a marginal degradation in
perplexity scaling (+0.78 points), it unlocks an exceptional 7.47× processing throughput speed enhancement factor
compared to native 32-bit deployments. Crucially, the 620 MB operational RAM envelope allows seamless
execution alongside active telemetry loops without triggering system out-of-memory kernel panics.
4. TEST VECTORS FOR MODEL PROMPT V ALIDATION

amless
execution alongside active telemetry loops without triggering system out-of-memory kernel panics.
4. TEST VECTORS FOR MODEL PROMPT V ALIDATION
For model parsing confirmation testing, the TinyLlama instance must ingest tactical JSON-like text strings and
generate structured responses without failing. The following test cases are used for evaluation:
Test Vector 01: Secure Command Extraction
Input Text: [ORD-2026] Alpha-3 Team proceed immediately to Grid-Coordinate
Alpha-Whiskey-4482. Maintain absolute RF silence until target parameters
established. Ack required.
DRDO Technical Report TR-2026-DEWT-089 2

Expected Structured Output: {"order_id": "ORD-2026", "unit": "Alpha-3",
"destination": "AW4482", "comms": "silent"}
Test Vector 02: Telemetry Data Degradation Parsing

ed Output: {"order_id": "ORD-2026", "unit": "Alpha-3",
"destination": "AW4482", "comms": "silent"}
Test Vector 02: Telemetry Data Degradation Parsing
Input Text: SYSTEM_ALERT: Battery cell at 14% on tactical node 09. External
ambient temperature 44C. Operational capacity degrading rapidly.
Expected Structured Output: {"alert_type": "hardware", "node": 9, "status":
"critical", "action": "power_save"}
5. CONCLUSION AND STRATEGIC HORIZONS
This technical report substantiates that sub-2B parameter LLMs like TinyLlama possess significant utility for
localized, secure, and isolated battlefield computing matrices. Future developmental efforts will prioritize
compiling specialized low-rank adaptation (LoRA) modules focused extensively on localized maritime and

. Future developmental efforts will prioritize
compiling specialized low-rank adaptation (LoRA) modules focused extensively on localized maritime and
aerospace signaling dialects to optimize edge-intelligence resilience frameworks.
REFERENCES
[1] Vaswani, A., et al., "Attention is All You Need," Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems , vol. 30, pp. 5998–
6008, 2017.
[2] Zhang, P., et al., "TinyLlama: An Open-Source Small Language Model," arXiv preprint arXiv:2401.02385 , 2024.
[3] Sundaram, A. K., "Low-Power Localized NLP Frameworks for Secure Tactical Communications Labs," DRDO Journal of
Electronic Systems , vol. 14, no. 2, pp. 112–125, 2025.
DRDO Technical Report TR-2026-DEWT-089 3
